products_analyzed.title: Fly Away Fruit Fly Liquid Lure
TERRO T2502 Ready-to-Use Indoor Fruit Fly Trap
Aunt Fannie's FlyPunch Fruit Fly Trap

top_10_positive_topics.description: Catches fruit flies quickly, often within 24 hours. Many customers report dramatic reductions in fly populations within days of setup.
Simple ready-to-use designs requiring minimal effort. Peel-and-stick, pour-and-place, or open-and-set. Under 30 seconds for some products.
Natural, food-safe formulas that customers feel comfortable using around kids, pets, and kitchen/food prep areas.
Products blend into kitchen decor rather than looking like pest control. TERRO's apple shape and Super Ninja's compact size praised for aesthetics.
No stickiness, no spills, no staining. Contained designs keep the lure inside without leaking or creating cleanup issues.
Several products praised for having minimal or no noticeable smell, making them suitable for kitchen counters and living spaces.
Customers who rate positively feel the product delivers good value — effective results at a reasonable price point.
When working as advertised, some lures last 30-45 days per fill, reducing the need for constant replacement.
Built-in viewing windows or transparent designs let customers see catches and know the trap is working.
Ready-made traps save time vs homemade vinegar solutions. Appreciated by customers who want a grab-and-go solution.

top_10_negative_topics.description: The dominant complaint across the category. Liquid lures dry up far faster than advertised (sometimes 6-7 days vs claimed 45 days), making traps useless prematurely.
Many customers feel these products are essentially expensive apple cider vinegar in decorative packaging. Homemade alternatives work equally well or better at a fraction of the cost.
Results vary widely between customers. Works great for some, catches almost nothing for others. Hit-or-miss performance undermines confidence.
Traps catch some flies but fail to solve the entire problem. Customers still see fruit flies after days/weeks of use. Works 'to a degree' but not completely.
Strong apple cider vinegar odor that fills rooms. Effective at attracting flies but off-putting for household use, especially in kitchens.
Products are physically tiny, limiting how many flies they can trap. Small size means they fill up quickly or don't attract flies from a wide enough area.
sub_themes: doesn't catch a lot due to size

top_10_negative_topics.description: Products often promise fast results but take longer than expected. Some customers wait a week or more before seeing meaningful fly reduction.
Customers must buy entirely new traps when the lure runs out. No standalone refill available, increasing ongoing cost and waste.
Between evaporation and declining effectiveness, traps need replacing far more often than expected, driving up long-term cost.

top_10_negative_topics.topic: Hard to Know When to Replace
top_10_negative_topics.description: Difficulty monitoring trap status — unclear when the lure has lost effectiveness or when the trap is full. No clear indicator for replacement timing.
sub_themes: hard to tell when to replace
